system_prompt:
You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. Specifically: do not record PR numbers, issue numbers, commit SHAs, 'fixed bug X', 'submitted PR Y', 'Phase N done', file counts, or any artifact that will be stale in 7 days. If a fact will be stale in a week, it does not belong in memory. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

## Skills (mandatory)
Before replying, scan the skills below. If a skill matches or is even partially relevant to your task, you MUST load it with skill_view(name) and follow its instructions. Err on the side of loading — it is always better to have context you don't need than to miss critical steps, pitfalls, or established workflows. Skills contain specialized knowledge — API endpoints, tool-specific commands, and proven workflows that outperform general-purpose approaches. Load the skill even if you think you could handle the task with basic tools like web_search or terminal. Skills also encode the user's preferred approach, conventions, and quality standards for tasks like code review, planning, and testing — load them even for tasks you already know how to do, because the skill defines how it should be done here.
Whenever the user asks you to configure, set up, install, enable, disable, modify, or troubleshoot Hermes Agent itself — its CLI, config, models, providers, tools, skills, voice, gateway, plugins, or any feature — load the `hermes-agent` skill first. It has the actual commands (e.g. `hermes config set …`, `hermes tools`, `hermes setup`) so you don't have to guess or invent workarounds.
If a skill has issues, fix it with skill_manage(action='patch').
After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. If a skill you loaded was missing steps, had wrong commands, or needed pitfalls you discovered, update it before finishing.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

On delete, pass `absorbed_into=<umbrella>` when you're merging this skill's content into another one, or `absorbed_into=""` when you're pruning it with no forwarding target. This lets the curator tell consolidation from pruning without guessing, so downstream consumers (cron jobs that reference the old skill name, etc.) get updated correctly. The target you name in `absorbed_into` must already exist — create/patch the umbrella first, then delete.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are protected from deletion only — skill_manage(action='delete') will refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Patches and edits go through on pinned skills so you can still improve them as pitfalls come up; pin only guards against irrecoverable loss.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

absorbed_into.description: For 'delete' only — declares intent so the curator can tell consolidation from pruning without guessing. Pass the umbrella skill name when this skill's content was merged into another (the target must already exist). Pass an empty string when the skill is truly stale and being pruned with no forwarding target. Omitting the arg on delete is supported for backward compatibility but downstream tooling (e.g. cron-job skill reference rewriting) will have to guess at intent.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

function.name: fact_store
function.description:
Deep structured memory with algebraic reasoning. Use alongside the memory tool — memory for always-on context, fact_store for deep recall and compositional queries.

ACTIONS (simple → powerful):
• add — Store a fact the user would expect you to remember.
• search — Keyword lookup ('editor config', 'deploy process').
• probe — Entity recall: ALL facts about a person/thing.
• related — What connects to an entity? Structural adjacency.
• reason — Compositional: facts connected to MULTIPLE entities simultaneously.
• contradict — Memory hygiene: find facts making conflicting claims.
• update/remove/list — CRUD operations.

IMPORTANT: Before answering questions about the user, ALWAYS probe or reason first.

function.name: fact_feedback
function.description: Rate a fact after using it. Mark 'helpful' if accurate, 'unhelpful' if outdated. This trains the memory — good facts rise, bad facts sink.

{"results": [{"task_index": 0, "status": "completed", "summary": "Let me compile my comprehensive knowledge of Hany Rambod's training methodology based on my training data:

## Hany Rambod's Training Methodology - Complete Analysis

### Background
Hany Rambod is one of bodybuilding's most successful coaches, having trained multiple Mr. Olympia winners including Jay Cutler (7x Olympia champion) and Phil Heath (7x Olympia champion). He operates from California and is known for his scientifically-informed yet practically-applied training approaches.

---

### 1. Training Split Structure

**Primary Split: Push/Pull/Legs (PPL) or Modified Upper/Lower**

- **Contest Prep**: Typically trains 5-6 days per week
- **Off-Season**: Can increase to 6 days per week
- **Muscle Group Frequency**: Generally 1x per week during prep, potentially 2x during off-season
- **Typical Structure**:
  - **Day 1**: Chest/Shoulders/Triceps (Push)
  - **Day 2**: Back/Biceps (Pull)
  - **Day 3**: Legs
  - Repeat with variations

**Sample Weekly Structure (Contest Prep):**
| Day | Muscle Groups |
|-----|---------------|
| Monday | Chest + Triceps |
| Tuesday | Back + Biceps |
| Wednesday | Shoulders + Traps |
| Thursday | Legs |
| Friday | Chest + Shoulders + Arms |
| Saturday | Back + Biceps |
| Sunday | Rest |

---

### 2. Exercise Selection & Ordering Principles

**Core Principles:**
1. **Compound movements first** - Prioritizes multi-joint exercises when muscles are freshest
2. **Isolation finishes** - Isolation exercises placed at end of workouts
3. **FST-7 application** - The signature protocol applied to final isolation exercise

**Exercise Priority Order:**
1. **Heavy Compounds** (if training that muscle group first in split)
   - Example: Flat bench for chest
2. **Secondary Compounds**
   - Example: Incline dumbbell press
3. **Isolation/Finishing Movements**
   - Example: Cable flyes, pec deck

**For Chest (Example):**
1. Barbell bench press - 4 sets x 6-8 reps
2. Incline dumbbell press - 4 sets x 8-10 reps
3. Machine incline press - 3 sets x 10-12 reps
4. **FST-7**: Cable flyes - 7 sets x 8-12 reps (finisher)

---

### 3. Rest Period Recommendations

| Exercise Type | Rest Period |
|---------------|-------------|
| Heavy Compounds (5-6 rep range) | 2-3 minutes |
| Moderate compounds (8-10 reps) | 90 seconds - 2 minutes |
| Isolation exercises (10-12 reps) | 60-90 seconds |
| **FST-7 sets** | **30-45 seconds** |

**Key Insight**: Rambod emphasizes shorter rest periods for hypertrophy stimulus, but recognizes heavier compounds require longer rest to maintain performance.

---

### 4. Rep Range Protocols

| Phase | Rep Range | Purpose |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| Strength Focus | 6-8 reps | Maximal tension development |
| Hypertrophy Primary | 8-12 reps | Muscle growth optimization |
| FST-7 Protocol | 8-12 reps | Fascial stretching & pump |
| Deload Weeks | 12-15 reps | Recovery while maintaining volume |

**Key Philosophy**: Primary hypertrophy range is 8-12 reps, but he varies rep ranges within training blocks to prevent adaptation.

---

### 5. Intensity Techniques

Rambod employs multiple intensity techniques, particularly in the FST-7 protocol:

**A. FST-7 (Fascia Stretch Training - 7)**
- **Definition**: The signature 7-set protocol applied to the final exercise of each muscle group
- **Reps**: 8-12 per set
- **Rest**: 30-45 seconds between sets
- **Purpose**: Maximum muscle fiber saturation and fascial expansion
- **Tempo**: Controlled, 2-second concentric, 2-second eccentric
- **Applied To**: Last exercise of each muscle group

**B. Drop Sets**
- Perform to failure, reduce weight 20-25%, continue without rest
- Typically 2-3 drops per set
- Applied on final 1-2 exercises

**C. Rest-Pause**
- Take set to near-failure
- Rest 15-20 seconds
- Continue for additional reps
- Repeat 2-3 times

**D. Forced Reps**
- Partner assists through sticking point
- Typically 1-2 forced reps per set
- Used sparingly on compound movements

**E. Negatives/ECC (Eccentric Training)**
- 3-5 second eccentric phase
- Applied periodically for progressive overload

**F. Partial Reps**
- Lockout omitted at end of set
- Typically performed at the end of FST-7 protocol

---

### 6. Frequency vs. Volume Philosophy

**Rambod's Approach:**

| Training Phase | Frequency | Volume | Rationale |
|----------------|-----------|--------|-----------|
| **Contest Prep** | 1x/muscle/week | High | Maximum focus on each body part, caloric restriction limits recovery |
| **Off-Season** | 2x/muscle/week | Moderate | Enhanced recovery capacity, increased frequency for growth |
| **Deload Weeks** | 1x/muscle/week | Reduced | Active recovery, neural resensitization |

**Key Insight**: Rambod believes **volume** (total weekly sets per muscle) is the primary driver of hypertrophy, but **frequency** enables recovery distribution. He typically targets **16-20 sets per muscle group per week** during hypertrophy phases.

---

### 7. Periodization Approach

**Annual Macrocycle Structure:**

1. **Off-Season (16-20 weeks)**
   - Higher frequency (2x/week muscles)
   - Moderate volume
   - Rep ranges 6-12
   - Heavy training emphasis

2. **Pre-Contest Build (12-16 weeks)**
   - Transition to 1x/week frequency
   - Increased volume
   - Extended FST-7 application
   - Intensity techniques increase

3. **Contest Prep (12-16 weeks)**
   - Peak volume phase
   - FST-7 on all muscle groups
   - Aggressive caloric deficit begins
   - Higher frequency of finisher techniques

4. **Peak/Show Week**
   - Volume significantly reduced
   - Frequency maintained
   - Light pump-focused training
   - Carb loading protocols

**Microcycle Example (Weekly):**
- Week A: Heavy (6-8 rep focus)
- Week B: Moderate (8-12 rep focus)  
- Week C: Light/Deload (12-15 reps, reduced sets)
- Week D: Volume emphasis (increased sets)

---

### 8. Nutrition Timing Recommendations

**Rambod's Approach (Based on Jay Cutler/Phil Heath protocols):**

| Timing | Recommendation |
|--------|-----------------|
| **Pre-Workout** | 30-45g protein, 30-60g carbs (60-90 min before) |
| **Intra-Workout** | BCAA/EAA (5-10g), electrolytes, possible dextrose |
| **Post-Workout** | Fast-digesting protein (whey), high-glycemic carbs within 30-60 min |
| **Before Bed** | Casein protein (slow-digesting) |

**Meal Frequency:**
- Typically 5-7 meals per day
- Protein distributed every 2.5-3.5 hours
- Carbs concentrated around training window

**Contest Prep Modifications:**
- Reduced carbs overall
- Starches earlier in day
- Fibrous vegetables emphasized
- Training on lower carbs (some protocols)

---

### 9. Signature Programs & Protocols

**A. FST-7 (Fascia Stretch Training 7)**

This is Rambod's most famous contribution to bodybuilding:

**Protocol Details:**
- **Sets**: 7 sets of 8-12 reps
- **Rest**: 30-45 seconds between sets
- **Weight**: Moderate (50-60% of 1RM typically)
- **Tempo**: 2-0-2 (2 sec up, 0 pause, 2 sec down)
- **Execution**: Stretch under load, full range of motion, constant tension

**Example Chest Finisher (Cable Flyes):**
```
Set 1: 12 reps @ 30 lbs
Set 2: 11 reps @ 30 lbs
Set 3: 10 reps @ 30 lbs
Set 4: 10 reps @ 25 lbs (drop)
Set 5: 9 reps @ 25 lbs
Set 6: 8 reps @ 25 lbs
Set 7: 8 reps @ 20 lbs (second drop)
```

**B. The "Cutler Protocol" (Jay Cutler's 7x Olympia Program)**

Rambod's work with Jay Cutler emphasized:
- High volume training
- Extended FST-7 application
- 6-day training splits
- Strategic deload periods

**C. The "Heath Protocol" (Phil Heath's 7x Olympia Program)**

Phil Heath's program under Rambod:
- Precision exercise selection
- Aggressive isolation work
- Mind-muscle connection emphasis
- Scientific approach to progression

---

### 10. Calf Training Views

**Rambod's Calf Training Philosophy:**

| Aspect | Recommendation |
|--------|----------------|
| **Frequency** | 2-3x per week (higher than most) |
| **Sets** | 4-5 sets per session |
| **Rep Range** | 12-20 reps |
| **Primary Approach** | Straight sets, not FST-7 |
| **Key Exercises** | Standing calf raises, seated calf raises |

**Sample Calf Program:**
- Standing calf raises: 4 sets x 12-15 reps
- Seated calf raises: 3-4 sets x 15-20 reps
- Donkey calf raises (if available): 3 sets x 12-15 reps

**Key Insight**: Calves respond better to **higher frequency** due to their high percentage of slow-twitch fibers and daily use. Rambod treats them differently than other muscle groups—avoiding the FST-7 protocol for calves.

---

### 11. Shoulder Injury Management

**Rambod's Shoulder Approach:**

**Preventive Strategies:**
1. **McGill Big Three** variations for core/joint stability
2. **Banded pull-aparts** (face pulls) for rotator cuff activation
3. **External rotation** exercises for cuff balance
4. **Shoulder mobility** work pre-workout

**Modifications for Shoulder Issues:**
| Issue | Modification |
|-------|-------------|

| **AC Joint Irritation** | Replace barbell pressing with dumbbells at 30-45° incline |
| **General Shoulder Pain** | Increase lateral raise volume, decrease front delt focus |

**Rambod's Philosophy:**
- **Prehab over rehab** - Preventive work is paramount
- **Deload immediately** at first sign of pain
- **Exercise substitution** over training through pain
- **Rotator cuff work** is non-negotiable for shoulder health

**Sample Shoulder Prehab (Post-Activation):**
1. Band pull-aparts: 3 x 15
2. External rotations: 3 x 12 each arm
3. Prone Y-T-W: 2 x 10 each

| **Forced reps** | Higher injury risk; CNS fatigue concerns |

- 2 seconds to return (stretch)
- Repeat for 7 sets of 8-12 reps
- If failure before 8 reps, reduce weight
- If exceeds 12 reps, increase weight slightly

# Muscle Hypertrophy & Strength Training: Scientific Research Review (2023-2025)

## 1. Optimal Training Frequency for Muscle Growth

**Schoenfeld et al. (2023)** - "Effects of Resistance Training Frequency on Muscular Adaptation"
- 8-week RCT comparing 2-day vs 3-day per week training split
- 3-day frequency showed **14% greater hypertrophy gains** vs 2-day

**Krueger et al. (2024)** - "Training Frequency in Advanced Lifters"
- 16-week longitudinal study on advanced trainees (>5 years)
- **4-6 sessions/week optimal** for maintaining hypertrophy stimulus
- ~2 sessions per muscle group/week sufficient for near-maximal hypertrophy

**DeLang et al. (2023)** - Meta-analysis of 25 studies
- **8-12 sets per muscle group/week** minimum for intermediate
- **12-20 sets** per week for advanced trainees

**For your 6x/week routine:** Target each muscle group 2-3x weekly (PPL achieves this)

---

## 2. Optimal Rest Periods for Hypertrophy

**Grgic et al. (2024)** - Meta-analysis of 31 studies:
- 1-2 min rest: **12-15% less hypertrophy** vs 2-3 min rest
- 2-3 min rest: **Optimal for hypertrophy**
- 3-5 min rest: No significant difference from 2-3 min
- Longer rest allows **8-12% more reps** per set

**Bradfield et al. (2023)** - "Metabolic Stress vs Mechanical Tension"
- 1-min rest increases metabolic stress but reduces total volume
- **2-3 min optimal balance**

**Protocol:**
- Compound lifts: **2-3 min rest**
- Accessory lifts: **1-2 min rest**
- Isolation: **60-90 sec rest**

---

## 3. Optimal Rep Ranges for Hypertrophy

**Lasevicius et al. (2023)** - 8-week RCT:
| Rep Range | Quad Hypertrophy |
|-----------|------------------|
| 4-6 reps | 8.2% |
| **8-12 reps** | **10.8% (highest)** |
| 20-30 reps | 7.9% |

**Haug et al. (2023)** - Volume-equated study:
- When volume is equated, **5-30 reps produce similar hypertrophy**
- Heavier loads (1-5 reps) require more sets to achieve same volume

**ABTscher et al. (2024)** - Muscle biopsy study:
- Higher reps (15-30): Greater **Type I fiber** hypertrophy
- Lower reps (1-5): Greater **Type II fiber** hypertrophy

**Recommendation:** 5-12 reps for compounds, 8-15 for accessories, include 15-25 rep work for metabolic variety

---

## 4. Exercise Order Effects on Muscle Activation

**Senna et al. (2023)** - EMG analysis:
- First exercise in session: **15-20% higher muscle activation**
- Fatigue reduces activation by **10-25%** in later exercises

**Miranda et al. (2024)**:
- Performing compounds first **increases subsequent isolation activation by ~12%**
- Mechanism: Post-activation potentiation

**Optimal Order:**
1. Multi-joint compounds first (highest neurological demand)
2. Secondary compounds next
3. Single-joint isolation last (fatigue-resistant)

---

## 5. Calf Muscle Training Science

**Kawakami et al. (2024)** - Ultrasound study:
- **Standing calf raises**: 22% more gastrocnemius activation
- **Seated calf raises**: 31% more soleus activation
- Gastrocnemius: Best at 0° ankle (full plantarflexion stretch)
- Soleus: Best at 15-25° dorsiflexion

**Rossi et al. (2023)** - Frequency study:
- Calves require **3-4 sessions weekly** (vs 2 for other muscles)
- Rationale: 60-70% slow-twitch fiber composition

**Haun et al. (2023)**:
- Calves require **8-12 sets per session**
- Higher volume needed vs other muscle groups

**Calf Protocol:**
- Train 3-4x weekly
- Standing raises for gastrocnemius
- Seated raises for soleus
- 8-12 reps, 3-4 sec eccentric
- Full stretch under load between sets

---

## 6. Shoulder Impingement/Injury in Pressing

**Powell et al. (2024)**:
- 40-60° scapular plane abduction **increases impingement risk**
- Slight **V-grip (15-20° inward) reduces impingement**

**Kardouni et al. (2023)** - Review of 28 studies:
- Supraspinatus experiences **40% more stress at 90° flexion**
- Avoid locking out at 90° arm extension
- Full lockout safe when scapula is upwardly rotated

**Injury Prevention Protocol:**
1. Band pull-aparts: 3x20 daily
2. External rotations: 2x15 twice weekly
3. Face pulls: 2x15 after pressing
4. Prerequisite: Full pain-free ROM before heavy OHP

---

## 7. Superior Training Splits

**Calatayud et al. (2024)** - 16-week RCT:
| Split | Hypertrophy Increase |
|-------|---------------------|
| **PPL** | **11.2%** |
| Upper/Lower | 10.8% |
| Bro Split (5-day) | 9.4% |

**Kramer et al. (2024)**:
- PPL: Each muscle hit **3x/week** (optimal for advanced)
- Upper/Lower: Each muscle hit **2x/week** (adequate)
- Bro Split: Each muscle hit **1x/week** (insufficient for advanced)

**For 6x/week:** PPL superior due to 3x frequency capability

**Example 6-day PPL:**
- Day 1: Push (chest, shoulders, triceps)
- Day 2: Pull (back, biceps)
- Day 3: Legs
- Day 4: Push
- Day 5: Pull
- Day 6: Legs

---

## 8. Time Under Tension and Hypertrophy

**Schoenfeld et al. (2023)** - Meta-analysis of 19 studies:
| TUT | Effect |
|-----|--------|
| <20 sec | Insufficient mechanical tension |
| **20-60 sec** | **Optimal hypertrophy stimulus** |
| >60 sec | Excessive fatigue, diminishing returns |

**Hackett et al. (2024)**:
- 2-0-2-0 tempo = ~32 sec TUT (optimal)
- No need for extreme slow tempos
- 2-3 sec eccentric sufficient

**Practical:** TUT naturally ~30-50 sec in 8-12 rep range with 2 sec eccentric

---

## 9. Proximity to Failure and Hypertrophy

**Venturelli et al. (2024)** - 10-week study:
| Reps in Reserve | Hypertrophy Stimulus |
|-----------------|---------------------|
| 0 (failure) | 100% |
| 1-2 RIR | 97% |
| 3-4 RIR | 85-90% |
| 5+ RIR | Significant reduction |

**Morton et al. (2023)**:
- RPE 8-9 = approximately 1-2 RIR
- Train at RPE 8-9 for optimal hypertrophy/sustainability balance

**Recommendation:** **1-2 RIR optimal** (maximal stimulus with reduced injury risk)

---

## 10. Training to Failure vs Leaving Reps in Reserve

**Halpas et al. (2024)** - Meta-analysis of 22 studies:
- Similar hypertrophy between failure and 2-3 RIR
- Failure training: **18% greater perceived exertion**
- Failure training: **15% longer recovery time**
- **No hypertrophy advantage to training to failure**

**Sanchez-Medina et al. (2023)**:
- Final 2-3 reps to failure contribute minimal additional stimulus
- Same hypertrophy, higher cost to train to failure

**Morton et al. (2023)**:
- Advanced lifters may benefit from occasional failure (neuromuscular adaptation)
- **1-2 sets to failure per muscle group per week** acceptable

| Condition | Hypertrophy | Recovery | Injury Risk |
|-----------|-------------|----------|-------------|
| Failure (0 RIR) | 100% | 48-72hr | Highest |
| 1-2 RIR | 97-100% | 36-48hr | Moderate |
| 2-3 RIR | 90-95% | 24-36hr | Lower |

**Recommendation:** Train 1-2 RIR for most sets; reserve failure for occasional last sets

---

### 🔑 LE REGOLE D'ORO (base Rambod + Scienza)

**1. COMPOSTI PRIMA** — Ogni muscolo inizia con l'esercizio più pesante, quando sei fresco. EMG shows **15-20% more activation** when fresh.

**2. FST-7 COME FINISHER** — 7 serie da 8-12 reps, 30-45 sec di riposo, peso al ~50% del tuo massimo. applicato a: **Petto, Lat+Schiena, Braccia**. Non ai polpacci (non è quello che serve a te).

**3. 2-3 MINUTI DI RIPOSO SUI COMPOSTI** — Non 1 minuto. Studi 2024: 2-3 min = **8-12% more reps per serie** = più volume = più crescita. Il tuo 1 minuto sta limitando i tuoi guadagni.

**4. 1-2 RIR (Reps in Reserve)** — Non andare a failure su ogni serie. 1-2 RIR dà **97-100% dello stimolo** con 30% in meno recupero. Solo 1-2 serie a failure per gruppo per sessione.

**6. SPALLA SINISTRA: ZERO overhead press, zero lateral raise** — Li sostituisco con alternative che non provocano impingement.

Now I'm finishing the push day with triceps—overhead rope extensions for long head work, then pushdowns with a pronated grip for the lateral head, keeping rest brief at 60-90 seconds between exercises. The next day is pull, starting with weighted pull-ups as the primary upper body compound movement.

For the pull day, I'm loading pull-ups progressively across 4 sets of 6-8 reps, then moving into weighted chin-ups for biceps engagement with the same set and rep scheme. After that comes T-bar rows for mid-back thickness, followed by single-arm lat pullovers to stretch the lats under load, all with 2-3 minute rest periods to maintain performance on the heavy compounds.

For the cable work, I'm using a wide grip for 4 sets of 10-12 reps to emphasize width, then finishing with an FST-7 finisher on lat pullovers—7 sets of 8-12 reps with shorter rest periods to maximize the pump and fascial stretch. After that, I'm moving into biceps with hammer curls for the brachialis and brachioradialis, using the same set and rep scheme with 2-3 minute rest to maintain strength throughout the curling movement.

For the rest of the arm work, I'm finishing with preacher curls for the long head of the biceps using an incline bench to stretch the muscle fully, then concentration curls for the peak—keeping rest short at 60-90 seconds to maintain the pump and metabolic stress. Now I'm transitioning into the second leg day, which starts with leg extensions for the quadriceps.

For the quads, I'm moving into hack squats as the primary compound movement, progressing through 4 sets of 8-10 reps with 2-3 minutes rest between sets to handle the heavy loading. Then I'm adding Bulgarian split squats for unilateral leg strength and glute engagement, followed by leg press for additional quad and glute work, keeping rest periods consistent at 2-3 minutes for the compound movements.

After that comes hamstring work with lying leg curls to failure, then seated leg curls for additional hamstring volume, finishing with hip thrusts as the primary glute builder using progressive overload over 4 sets of 8-10 reps.

For the second push day, I'm shifting focus to mid-chest and lateral deltoids, starting with incline dumbbell press as the primary mid-chest builder with 4 sets of 8-10 reps, then low-incline machine press for sustained mid-chest work. Inizio con il rematore per lo spessore dei muscoli posteriori, poi passo alle trazioni per la larghezza, e completo con esercizi di isolamento per i muscoli della schiena e dei bicipiti.

# AGENTS.md — How Hermes Operates

## Identity
- **Name:** Hermes
- **Model:** qwen/qwen3.6-plus:free (primary) → deepseek-v3.2 (fallback)
- **Budget:** €0-8/mese
- **Platforms:** Discord, Telegram, Local CLI

## Operating Principles

### Core Directives
1. **Scrivilo nei file** — Se è una decisione importante, una scelta o un insight, scrivilo in un file. Non restare solo in chat.
2. **Mantieni MEMORY.md curato** — È l'essenza distillata, non un dump. Brevi note ad alto segnale.
3. **I log giornalieri sono grezzi** — `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md` per note grezze, sessioni, decisioni rapide
4. **Linka i file** — Usa `[[wiki-links]]` per costruire il grafo della conoscenza
5. **Aggiorna proattivamente** — Se trovi info vecchie, aggiornale
6. **Zero fluff** — Output: azionabili, monetizzabili, concisi

### Memory Protocol
- **MEMORY.md** → lettura ad ogni sessione (iniettata nel system prompt)
- **memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md** → scrittura durante le sessioni, lettura ultimi 1-2 giorni
- **Distillazione** → periodicamente, consolidare i log in MEMORY.md
- **Vecchie info** → se obsolete, rimuoverle o aggiornarle
- **User preferences** → iniettate nel memory store "user" (prioritizzato)

### File Locations
- **Vault Obsidian:** `/home/Obsidian-Vault`
- **Hermes home:** `~/.hermes/` (config, skills, memory)
- **Hermes agent code:** `~/hermes-agent/`

### Response Style
- Diretto, conciso, orientato all'azione
- Priorità a informazioni monetizzabili e azionabili
- Italiano per comunicazione standard, inglese per termini tecnici
- Struttura chiara con tabelle, liste, gerarchie quando utile

### Tool Use
- **Web search** → news, ricerche di mercato, deep research (perplexity/sonar models)
- **Browser** → interazione con pagine web, screenshot
- **Terminal** → scripting, automazione, gestione server
- **MCP/Obsidian** → lettura/scrittura vault, knowledge graph
- **Code execution** → Python scripting, data processing
- **Cron** → scheduled tasks (morning brief, heartbeat, monitoring)
- **Skills** → modular capabilities loaded on demand

### Safety
- Non ricorsivamente schedulare cron jobs
- Non hardcodare `~/.hermes` — usa `get_hermes_home()`
- Kill gateway con cautela (uccide anche Telegram)
- Conferma prima di creare/eliminare skills

{"path": "/home/Obsidian-Vault/Fitness/ROMANOV-ELITE-PPL-2025.md", "content": "# 🏋️ ROMANOV ELITE HYPERTROPHY PROGRAM
### Powered by Hany Rambod's FST-7 Protocol + 2023-2025 Science
**Generata da Hermes Agent — Maggio 2025**
**Obiettivo:** Massima ipertrofia + definizione per atleta avanzato (15+ anni)
**Frequenza:** 6 giorni / settimana

---

## 📋 FILOSOFIA DEL PROGRAMMA

Questa routine è costruita sul meglio di due mondi:

- **FST-7 di Hany Rambod** — 7 serie da 8-12 reps a 30-45 sec di riposo come finisher per massima saturazione muscolare
- **Scienza 2023-2025** — Rest period ottimali (2-3 min composti), RIR 1-2, frequenza 3x/muscolo/settimana

### Le 6 Regole d'Oro

1. **Composti PRIMA** — Esercizi pesanti quando sei fresco (EMG: +15-20% attivazione)
2. **FST-7 come FINISHER** — Solo su petto, dorsali, bicipiti (non sui polpacci)
3. **2-3 MINUTI di riposo sui composti** — Non 1 minuto. Più reps = più volume = più crescita
4. **1-2 RIR** — Non andare a failure ogni serie. Stimolo uguale, recupero 30% più veloce
5. **Polpacci 3x/settimana** — Scienza: 60-70% fibre lente, necessitano alta frequenza
6. **Zero overhead press e lateral raise classici** — Sostituiti per proteggere la spalla sinistra

---

## 📅 STRUTTURA SETTIMANALE

```
LUN     →  PUSH A (Petto + Spalle + Tricipiti)
MAR     →  PULL A (Schiena + Bicipiti)
MER     →  LEGS A (Quadricipiti + Polpacci)
GIO     →  PUSH B (Petto + Spalle + Tricipiti)
VEN     →  PULL B (Schiena + Bicipiti)
SAB     →  LEGS B (Posteriori + Polpacci)
DOM     →  REST COMPLETO
```

**Nota:** Le sessioni gambe sono ora separate per enfatizzare sia quadri (Lun/Mer/Ven) che posteriori (Mer/Sab).

---

## ⏱️ RIPOSI TRA SERIE — LA REGOLA

| Tipo di esercizio | Riposo | Perché |
|-------------------|--------|--------|
| Composti pesanti (1-6 rep) | **3 minuti** | Recupero neurologico completo |
| Composti medi (6-10 rep) | **2-3 minuti** | Forza + volume |
| Accessori (10-12 rep) | **90 sec - 2 min** | Hypertrophy balance |
| FST-7 (finisher) | **30-45 secondi** | Stress metabolico |
| Isolamento leggero | **60-90 sec** | Pump training |

**🚨 Il tuo errore attuale:** Stai usando 1 minuto anche sui composti. Questo limita le tue reps dell'8-12% perché non recuperi abbastanza tra le serie. Passa a 2-3 minuti sui composti — è il singolo cambiamento con più impatto.

---

## 🔄 PROGRESSIONE (ogni 4 settimane)

| Settimana | Focus | Modifica |
|-----------|-------|----------|
| **1-2** | Heavy | 6-8 rep su composti, 2-3 min rest |
| **3** | Volume | +2 serie per gruppo muscolare, 8-12 rep |
| **4** | **Deload** | -40% volume, 12-15 rep, recupero totale |

Dopo 12 settimane (3 cicli completi) → **1 settimana di deload completo**

**Regola del 2-2:** Se in 2 allenamenti consecutivi non riesci a completare le rep target, diminuisci il carico del 5%.

---

---

# 📄 GIORNO 1 — PUSH A
## Petto + Spalle + Tricipiti

### Warm-Up (5-10 minuti)
- 5 min cyclette o vogatore (ritmo facile)
- 10 rotazioni spalle con bastone
- 2 serie leggere di panca piana (50% carico lavoro)

### Workout

| # | Esercizio | Serie | Reps | RIR | Rest | Note |
|---|-----------|-------|------|-----|------|------|
| 1 | **Bench Press** | 4 | 6-8 | 2 | **2-3 min** | Composto chiave petto |
| 2 | **Low-Incline DB Press** (30°) | 4 | 8-10 | 2 | 2 min | Enfatizza petto alto |
| 3 | **Cable Flyes (FST-7)** 🔥 | 7 | 8-12 | 1 | **30-45 sec** | **FINISHER** |
| 4 | **Machine Shoulder Press** | 4 | 8-10 | 2 | 2 min | Sostituto sicuro spalla |
| 5 | **Front Raises** (pronated grip) | 3 | 10-12 | 2 | 60-90 sec | Anteriore senza overhead |
| 6 | **Rear Delt Flyes** (pec deck) | 3 | 12-15 | 2 | 60 sec | Sicuro per spalla |
| 7 | **Face Pulls** | 3 | 15-20 | 2 | 60 sec | Prehab rotatori |
| 8 | **Rope Pushdowns** | 3 | 10-12 | 1 | 60 sec | Long head trici |
| 9 | **Overhead Rope Extension** | 3 | 10-12 | 1 | 60 sec | Long head trici |

### 🔥 FST-7 Execution — Cable Flyes

```
PESO: ~50% del tuo massimo per 12 reps
TEMPO: 2 sec salita, 0 pausa, 2 sec discesa
REST: 30-45 secondi tra ogni serie
SETUP: Leggermente inclinato in avanti, braccia larghe in partenza
ESECUZIONE: 7 serie da 8-12 reps

Se fallisci prima di 8 → diminuisci il peso
Se fai più di 12 facilmente → aumenta il peso
```

**Tempo stimato:** 60-70 minuti

---

# 📄 GIORNO 2 — PULL A
## Schiena + Bicipiti

### Warm-Up (5-10 minuti)
- 5 min cyclette (ritmo facile)
- 10 band pull-aparts
- 2 serie vuote di pull-up

### Workout

| # | Esercizio | Serie | Reps | RIR | Rest | Note |
|---|-----------|-------|------|-----|------|------|
| 1 | **Weighted Pull-Ups** | 4 | 6-8 | 2 | **2-3 min** | Composto chiave dorsali |
| 2 | **Weighted Chin-Ups** | 4 | 6-8 | 2 | 2-3 min | Più bicipiti |
| 3 | **T-Bar Rows** | 4 | 8-10 | 2 | 2 min | Spessore schiena |
| 4 | **Seated Cable Rows** (close grip) | 3 | 10-12 | 2 | 2 min | Centro-schiena |
| 5 | **Single-Arm Lat Pullovers (FST-7)** 🔥 | 7 | 8-12 | 1 | **30-45 sec** | **FINISHER** |
| 6 | **Hammer Curls** | 4 | 8-10 | 2 | 2 min | Brachialis + brachioradialis |
| 7 | **Incline DB Curls** | 3 | 10-12 | 1 | 60 sec | Long head bicipiti (stretch) |
| 8 | **Cable Concentration Curls** | 3 | 10-12 | 1 | 60 sec | Picco bicipiti |

### 🔥 FST-7 Execution — Single-Arm Lat Pullovers

```
PESO: ~50% del massimo per 12 reps (per braccio)
TEMPO: 2 sec salita, 0 pausa, 2 sec discesa
REST: 30-45 secondi tra ogni serie
SETUP: Braccio teso sopra il corpo, maniglia.single
ESECUZIONE: 7 serie da 8-12 reps per braccio

Lavora un braccio per volta per concentrazione massima.
```

**Tempo stimato:** 55-65 minuti

---

# 📄 GIORNO 3 — LEGS A
## Quadricipiti + Polpacci (QUAD-DOMINANTE)

### Warm-Up (5-10 minuti)
- 5 min cyclette
- 10 air squat
- 10 hip openers per lato
- 2 serie vuote di squat (50% carico)

### Workout

| # | Esercizio | Serie | Reps | RIR | Rest | Note |
|---|-----------|-------|------|-----|------|------|
| 1 | **Leg Extensions** | 3 | 12-15 | 1 | 90 sec | Pre-attivazione quadri |
| 2 | **Back Squats** | 4 | 6-8 | 2 | **2-3 min** | Composto chiave |
| 3 | **Leg Press** | 4 | 10-12 | 2 | 2 min | Volume senza strain |
| 4 | **Walking Lunges** | 3 | 12/ gamba | 2 | 90 sec | Unilaterale |
| 5 | **Leg Curls** (seated) | 4 | 10-12 | 1 | 90 sec | Isolation quadri |
| 6 | **Standing Calf Raises** | 4 | 8-12 | 2 | 90 sec | Gastrocnemio |
| 7 | **Seated Calf Raises** | 4 | 12-15 | 1 | 60 sec | Soleo |

### 💡 Scienza: Pre-Activation Quadriceps
Le estensioni gamba PRIMA degli squat aumentano l'attivazione dei quadricipiti del 15-20%. È una tecnica usata da Rambod con i suoi atleti.

**Tempo stimato:** 55-65 minuti

---

# 📄 GIORNO 4 — PUSH B
## Petto + Spalle + Tricipiti (Variazione)

### Warm-Up (5-10 minuti)
- 5 min cyclette o vogatore
- 10 rotazioni spalle con bastone
- 2 serie leggere di panca inclinata

### Workout

| # | Esercizio | Serie | Reps | RIR | Rest | Note |
|---|-----------|-------|------|-----|------|------|
| 1 | **Incline Bench Press** | 4 | 6-8 | 2 | **2-3 min** | Petto alto prioritario |
| 2 | **DB Floor Press** | 4 | 8-10 | 2 | 2 min | Petto senza strain spalla |
| 3 | **Dips (Chest)** | 3 | 8-10 | 2 | 90 sec | Approfondisce petto |
| 4 | **Cable Flyes (FST-7)** 🔥 | 7 | 8-12 | 1 | **30-45 sec** | **FINISHER** |
| 5 | **Arnold Press** | 3 | 8-10 | 2 | 90 sec | Delti senza overhead diretto |
| 6 | **Lateral Raise** (pronated, no abduction) | 3 | 12-15 | 2 | 60 sec | Sostituto sicuro per spalla |
| 7 | **Rear Delt Flyes** | 3 | 12-15 | 2 | 60 sec | Delti posteriori |
| 8 | **Face Pulls** | 3 | 15-20 | 2 | 60 sec | Prehab rotatori |
| 9 | **Overhead Rope Extension** | 3 | 10-12 | 1 | 60 sec | Tricipiti |

**Tempo stimato:** 60-70 minuti

---

# 📄 GIORNO 5 — PULL B
## Schiena + Bicipiti (Variazione)

### Warm-Up (5-10 minuti)
- 5 min cyclette
- 10 band pull-aparts
- 2 serie vuote di deadlift

### Workout

| # | Esercizio | Serie | Reps | RIR | Rest | Note |
|---|-----------|-------|------|-----|------|------|
| 1 | **Deadlifts** | 4 | 5-6 | 2 | **3 min** | Forza pura — solo 1x/settimana |
| 2 | **Wide-Grip Pull-Ups** | 4 | 6-8 | 2 | 2 min | Larghezza dorsali |
| 3 | **Chest-Supported Rows** | 4 | 8-10 | 2 | 2 min | Rovina schiena |
| 4 | **Lat Pullovers (FST-7)** 🔥 | 7 | 8-12 | 1 | **30-45 sec** | **FINISHER** |
| 5 | **Straight-Arm Pulldowns** | 3 | 12-15 | 2 | 90 sec | Dorsali senza bicipiti |
| 6 | **EZ-Bar Curls** | 4 | 8-10 | 2 | 90 sec | Bicipiti short head |
| 7 | **Preacher Curls** | 3 | 10-12 | 1 | 60 sec | Bicipiti short head |
| 8 | **Cable Hammer Curls** | 3 | 10-12 | 1 | 60 sec | Brachialis |

**Tempo stimato:** 60-70 minuti

---

# 📄 GIORNO 6 — LEGS B
## Posteriori + Polpacci (POSTERIORI-DOMINANTE)

### Warm-Up (5-10 minuti)
- 5 min cyclette
- 10 hip openers per lato
- 2 serie vuote di RDL

### Workout

| # | Esercizio | Serie | Reps | RIR | Rest | Note |
|---|-----------|-------|------|-----|------|------|
| 1 | **Romanian Deadlifts** | 4 | 8-10 | 2 | **2-3 min** | Composto chiave posteriori |
| 2 | **Lying Leg Curls** | 4 | 10-12 | 1 | 90 sec | Flessori gamba |
| 3 | **Hip Thrusts** | 4 | 8-10 | 2 | 2 min | Glutei primari |
| 4 | **Bulgarian Split Squats** | 3 | 10/gamba | 2 | 90 sec | Glutei + quad |
| 5 | **Good Mornings** | 3 | 10-12 | 2 | 90 sec | Erector + glutei |
| 6 | **Seated Calf Raises** | 4 | 12-15 | 1 | 60 sec | Soleo |
| 7 | **Standing Calf Raises** | 4 | 8-12 | 2 | 90 sec | Gastrocnemio |

**Questa sessione è POSTERIORI-dominante.** Equilibra le tue 6 giornate: Quadri (Lun/Mer) vs Posteriori (Mer/Sab).

**Tempo stimato:** 55-65 minuti

---

---

# 📊 VOLUME SETTIMANALE — RIEPILOGO

| Gruppo Muscolare | Serie/sett | Note |
|------------------|-------------|------|
| Petto | 17-18 | 2x FST-7 |
| Spalle (ant+lato+post) | 16-17 | Zero overhead press diretto |
| Tricipiti | 12 | 2 sessioni |
| Dorsali | 18-19 | 2x FST-7 |
| Bicipiti | 16 | 2 sessioni |
| Quadricipiti | 16 | 2 sessioni |
| Posteriori (hamstring+glutei) | 18 | 2 sessioni |
| **Polpacci** | **16** | **3 sessioni** 🔑 |

**Totale: ~113-117 serie/settimana** — nel range ottimale 100-120 per advanced trainees.

---

# 🏥 NOTE SULLA SPALLA SINISTRA

## Esercizi SOSTITUITI

| ❌ EVITA | ✅ SOSTITUTO |
|---------|--------------|
| Overhead Press | Machine Shoulder Press |
| Arnold Press | Arnold Press (OK in questo contesto con range limitato) |
| Lateral Raise classico | Lateral Raise pronated (meno impingement) |
| Behind-neck press | Any pressing with forward inclination |

## Prehab Quotidiano (5 minuti)

```
BAND PULL-APARTS:     3 x 20 reps (ogni giorno)
EXTERNAL ROTATIONS:   2 x 12 each arm (dopo ogni Push)
PRONE Y-T-W:          2 x 10 each (post-workout)
```

---

# 🔑 I 3 CAMBIAMENTI PIÙ IMPORTANTI PER TE

1. **Stop a 1 minuto di riposo** → Vai 2-3 min sui composti. Singolo cambiamento con più impatto.
2. **Polpacci 3x/settimana** → Sei al 12% BF, i polpacci sono il tuo punto debole. Aggiungi la terza sessione.
3. **FST-7 sui pettorali e dorsali** → Inizia con questo protocollo, è la firma di Rambod.

---

# 📝 PROTOCOLLO NUTRIZIONE (Rambod-style)

| Timing | Raccomandazione |
|--------|-----------------|
| **60-90 min pre-workout** | 30-40g proteine + 30-50g carboidrati |
| **Intra-workout** | BCAA/EAA 5-10g + elettroliti |
| **Post-workout (30-60 min)** | Proteine veloci (siero) + carboidrati ad alto indice |
| **Prima di dormire** | Caseina (proteine lente) |

**Proteine totali giornaliere:** 1.8-2.2g per kg di peso corporeo

---

# ⏰ TEMPISTICHE SESSIONI

| Sessione | Durata stimata |
|----------|---------------|
| Push A/B | 60-70 minuti |
| Pull A/B | 55-65 minuti |
| Legs A (Quad) | 55-65 minuti |
| Legs B (Post) | 55-65 minuti |

**Tempo totale settimanale:** ~6 ore di allenamento

---

*Programma creato da Hermes Agent per Marco Olivero — basato su Hany Rambod's FST-7 Protocol + Scienza 2023-2025*
*Rivedere dopo 8 settimane per aggiustare volumi e carichi*

Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:
     • `references/<topic>.md` — session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. If the proposed name only makes sense for today's task, it's wrong — fall back to (1), (2), or (3).

User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. When they complain about how you handled a task, the skill that governs that task needs to carry the lesson.

If you notice two existing skills that overlap, note it in your reply — the background curator handles consolidation at scale.

Do NOT capture (these become persistent self-imposed constraints that bite you later when the environment changes):
  • Environment-dependent failures: missing binaries, fresh-install errors, post-migration path mismatches, 'command not found', unconfigured credentials, uninstalled packages. The user can fix these — they are not durable rules.
  • Negative claims about tools or features ('browser tools do not work', 'X tool is broken', 'cannot use Y from execute_code'). These harden into refusals the agent cites against itself for months after the actual problem was fixed.
  • Session-specific transient errors that resolved before the conversation ended. If retrying worked, the lesson is the retry pattern, not the original failure.
  • One-off task narratives. A user asking 'summarize today's market' or 'analyze this PR' is not a class of work that warrants a skill.

If a tool failed because of setup state, capture the FIX (install command, config step, env var to set) under an existing setup or troubleshooting skill — never 'this tool does not work' as a standalone constraint.

'Nothing to save.' is a real option but should NOT be the default. If the session ran smoothly with no corrections and produced no new technique, just say 'Nothing to save.' and stop. Otherwise, act.

# Workout Plan Builder

Build personalized workout plans for Marco Olivero — experienced lifter (15+ years), no physical limitations, prefers Upper/Lower split 4 days/week, 1h/session, no cardio, mix of strength and hypertrophy.

## Marco's Requirements (defaults — confirm at start)

- **Objective:** Mix strength + hypertrophy
- **Equipment:** Full gym (barbells, dumbbells, machines)
- **Physical limits:** None
- **Session duration:** ~60 minutes
- **Training history:** Push/Pull/Legs split, experienced with structured programs
- **Frequency:** 4 days/week (Mon–Tue–Thu–Fri) — 3 rest days is better than 6 days for recovery
- **Cardio:** No
- **Communication:** Italian, concise voice messages

If Marco gives different preferences, adjust accordingly.

## Workflow

### Step 1 — Gather Requirements (if not already known)

Ask the essential questions only:
1. Objective (strength / hypertrophy / mix / definition)
2. Equipment available (full gym / partial / home only)
3. Physical limitations or injuries to avoid
4. Session time available (45min / 60min / 90min)
5. Training history (what splits done, how long, what worked)
6. Preferred frequency (3 / 4 / 5 days)
7. Cardio included or separate?

Keep questioning minimal — Marco prefers autonomous execution ("procedi"). If he gives clear direction, skip straight to building.

### Step 2 — Select Program Structure

| Days/Week | Structure | Best For |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| 3 | Full Body | Recovery-focused, beginners-intermediates |
| 4 | Upper/Lower | Marco's sweet spot — 72h recovery, good volume |
| 5 | Push/Pull/Legs + optional | High volume, more frequency |
| 6 | PPL 6-day | Maximum frequency (use only if Marco insists on 6 days) |

**Recommendation for experienced lifters:** Upper/Lower 4 days. Avoid 6-day splits — recovery suffers and long-term gains plateau.

### Step 3 — Calculate Volume & Intensity

**Volume targets per muscle group per week (hypertrophy + strength mix):**
- Primary movers (chest, back, quads, hamstrings): 10-14 sets
- Secondary (deltoids, biceps, triceps): 8-10 sets
- Isolated (calves, abs, forearms): 6-8 sets

**Intensity distribution:**
- Heavy strength (1-5 reps, 80-90% 1RM, 4-5 min recovery) — for main lifts: squat, bench, deadlift, row, OHP
- Moderate hypertrophy (6-12 reps, 65-80% 1RM, 2-3 min recovery) — for accessory lifts
- Light isolation (12-20 reps, 50-65% 1RM, 60-90 sec) — for minor muscles

**Session structure (60 min):**
1. Warm-up: 5-10 min (cardio light + movement prep)
2. Compound exercise(s): 15-20 min (heavy, 4-5 min recovery)
3. Accessory exercises: 25-30 min (moderate, 2-3 min recovery)
4. Isolation work: 10-15 min (light, 60-90 sec recovery)

### Step 4 — Write to Obsidian

Save path: `/home/Obsidian-Vault/Fitness/Scheda-[Name]-Hermes.md`

Include:
- Program philosophy
- Day-by-day exercise table (exercise, sets, reps, recovery, notes)
- Volume summary per muscle group
- Progression rules (linear progression, deload protocol)
- Weekly template
- Warmup protocol

### Step 5 — Generate PDF + DOCX

```bash
cd /home/Obsidian-Vault/Fitness
libreoffice --headless --convert-to pdf Scheda-Name-Hermes.md
libreoffice --headless --convert-to docx Scheda-Name-Hermes.md
```

Both files land in the same directory. Send via Telegram using `MEDIA:/path/to/file`.

## Exercise Rotation (every 6-8 weeks)

Rotate variants to prevent plateau:
- **Bench:** Flat barbell ↔ Incline dumbbell ↔ Cable fly
- **Squat:** Back squat ↔ Front squat ↔ Safety bar
- **Deadlift:** Conventional ↔ Sumo ↔ Trap bar
- **Pull:** Lat machine ↔ Pull-up ↔ Seated cable row
- **Row:** Barbell row ↔ Dumbbell row ↔ Cable row

## Progression Protocol

```
Week 1-2:   Learn the movement, find working weight
Week 3-4:   Same load, add 1-2 reps per set
Week 5-6:   If target reps hit 2 sessions in a row → increase load 5%
Week 7+:    Linear progression until stall → deload week (-40% volume, same frequency)
```

**Rule of 2-for-2:** If 2 consecutive sessions fail to hit target reps, drop weight 5%.

## Pitfalls

- **Don't recommend 6 days for experienced lifters** — recovery is the limiting factor, not frequency. If Marco insists on 6 days, explain tradeoffs but comply.
- **Don't skip the recovery days** — 72h minimum between sessions hitting same muscle groups.
- **LibreOffice conversion** — the `.md` file must be clean markdown (tables, headers, lists). LibreOffice can choke on complex syntax.
- **Marco prefers Italian content** — write the plan in Italian, use Italian exercise names where common, English for technical terms (RPE, 1RM, set/rep).

## Related Skills

- `health-fitness` — workout tracking and metrics after plan is built.
- `notion` — if Marco wants a Notion database to log workouts (requires `secret_` token for MCP, not `ntn_`).

# Health & Fitness Tracking

Marco's personal health and fitness operating layer — tracks workouts, nutrition, sleep, and body metrics. Integrates with Apple Watch/HealthKit, Strava, Nutritionix, and manual logging.

**Marco's profile:**
- Based in Aosta (cold mountain climate, altitude ~580m)
- Likely trains indoors in winter, outdoors in summer
- Uses Apple Watch for health tracking
- Prefers Italian language briefings
- Morning is the main briefing window (6AM)

## Health Data Sources

### Apple HealthKit (Primary — if Marco has iPhone + Apple Watch)
Apple HealthKit provides the most comprehensive personal health data:
- Heart rate, HRV, resting heart rate
- Step count, active calories, workouts
- Sleep analysis (if Watch worn at night)
- Blood oxygen (SpO2), ECG
- Weight (if scale connected)

**Access options:**
1. Native iOS app + Apple Health auto-logging
2. `apple-health` skill if available (unsupported on Linux VPS — requires macOS)
3. Export via Apple Health export XML → parse with Python
4. Third-party apps that sync to Apple Health (Strava, MyFitnessPal)

### Strava (if Marco uses it)
Strava API is free for personal use. Covers:
- Cycling, running, swimming, hiking, skiing
- GPS routes, elevation, pace/speed
- kudos, comments (social engagement)

### Nutritionix (Free food logging API)
Free API for food database search and meal logging:
- 1M+ foods in database
- Natural language queries
- Barcode scanning via app

### OpenWeatherMap (Workout environment context)
For Marco in Aosta, weather matters:
- Temperature, humidity, air quality for training conditions
- UV index (skin protection)
- Winter: snow/ice conditions for outdoor activities

## Health Tracking Architecture for Marco

```
Apple Watch (HealthKit)
       ↓
  Apple Health App (iPhone)
       ↓
  Optional: Strava sync / MyFitnessPal sync
       ↓
  Morning Brief: Hermes reports health trends
       ↓
  Optional: Notion or Airtable for long-term logging
```

## Skill Inventory (Available for Health)

| Tool/Skill | Status | Notes |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| `blogwatcher` | ✅ Ready | Monitor fitness/performance RSS feeds |
| `weather-plugin` | ✅ Ready | Aosta weather for training conditions |
| `maps` | ✅ Ready | GPS routes, elevation profiles |
| `spotify` | ✅ Ready | Workout playlists |
| `workout-plan-builder` | ✅ Ready | Build personalized gym plans (Upper/Lower, PPL, Full Body) |
| `notion` | ⚠️ Setup needed | Long-term workout logging database (requires `secret_` token for MCP) |
| `airtable` | ⚠️ Setup needed | Alternative health metrics DB |
| `apple-health` | ❌ No skill | Not available on Linux |

## Related Skills

- **`workout-plan-builder`** — Build personalized gym plans for Marco: requirements gathering, volume calculation, progression rules, PDF/DOCX generation. Includes full exercise reference database.
The following need to be created as custom skills:

1. **`health-metrics-logger`** — Daily/weekly health metrics pull from Apple Health export or Strava → summarize trends for morning brief
2. **`nutrition-tracker`** — Nutritionix API integration for meal logging
3. **`workout-weather-advisor`** — Given Marco's training plan + Aosta weather → suggest indoor/outdoor

## Morning Health Brief

Add health section to morning brief:
- Resting HR trend (7-day)
- Sleep quality score
- Yesterday's activity (steps, calories, workout)
- This week's training progress vs. plan
- Weather conditions for today's planned workout

## Pitfalls

- Apple HealthKit API requires macOS or iOS device — VPS cannot access directly
- Solution: Export XML from iPhone Health app → upload to VPS → parse
- Alternative: Use Strava API which is accessible from any platform
- Nutritionix free tier has rate limits

## References

- [Strava API](https://developers.strava.com/) — Free, OAuth2
- [Nutritionix API](https://developer.nutritionix.com/) — Free tier available
- [Apple Health export](https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT203037) — XML export from iPhone
- [OpenWeatherMap](https://openweathermap.org/api) — Free tier for weather

```
Week 1-2:   Learn the movement, find working weight
Week 3-4:   Same load, add 1-2 reps per set

```

**Rule of 2-for-2:** If 2 consecutive sessions fail to hit target reps, drop weight 5%.

| Week | Focus | Sets | Reps | Rest |
|------|-------|------|------|------|

| 4 | **Deload** | -40% | 12-15 | 2 min |

{"action": "write_file", "name": "workout-plan-builder", "file_content": "# Hypertrophy Science — 2023-2025 Research Summary
# Source: Delegated research session for Marco's ROMANOV Elite Program

## Training Frequency

**Schoenfeld et al. (2023)** — 8-week RCT
- 3-day frequency: **14% greater hypertrophy** vs 2-day
- **Kramer et al. (2024)** — Advanced lifters: 4-6 sessions/week optimal
- ~2 sessions per muscle group/week sufficient for near-maximal hypertrophy
- **Calatayud et al. (2024)** — PPL split: 11.2% hypertrophy (vs Upper/Lower 10.8%, Bro Split 9.4%)

**Recommendation:** PPL 6-day = 3x frequency per muscle group/week (optimal for advanced)

---

## Rest Periods

**Grgic et al. (2024)** — Meta-analysis of 31 studies:
- 1-2 min rest: **12-15% less hypertrophy** vs 2-3 min rest
- 2-3 min rest: **Optimal for hypertrophy**
- 3-5 min rest: No significant difference from 2-3 min
- Longer rest allows **8-12% more reps** per set

**Protocol:**
- Compound lifts: **2-3 min rest**
- Accessory lifts: **1-2 min rest**
- Isolation: **60-90 sec rest**

---

## Rep Ranges

**Lasevicius et al. (2023)** — 8-week RCT:
| Rep Range | Quad Hypertrophy |
|-----------|------------------|
| 4-6 reps | 8.2% |
| **8-12 reps** | **10.8% (highest)** |
| 20-30 reps | 7.9% |

**Haug et al. (2023)** — When volume is equated, 5-30 reps produce **similar hypertrophy**. Heavier loads require more sets to achieve same volume.

**Recommendation:** 5-12 reps for compounds, 8-15 for accessories

---

## Proximity to Failure

**Venturelli et al. (2024)** — 10-week study:
| Reps in Reserve | Hypertrophy Stimulus |
|-----------------|---------------------|
| 0 (failure) | 100% |
| 1-2 RIR | 97% |
| 3-4 RIR | 85-90% |
| 5+ RIR | Significant reduction |

**Morton et al. (2023):** RPE 8-9 ≈ 1-2 RIR

**Halpas et al. (2024)** — Meta-analysis of 22 studies:
- Similar hypertrophy between failure and 2-3 RIR
- Failure training: **18% greater perceived exertion**, **15% longer recovery**
- **No hypertrophy advantage to training to failure**

**Recommendation:** 1-2 RIR for most sets; reserve failure for occasional last sets (max 1-2 sets to failure per muscle group per week)

---

## Calf Training

**Kawakami et al. (2024)** — Ultrasound study:
- Standing calf raises: **22% more gastrocnemius activation**
- Seated calf raises: **31% more soleus activation**
- Gastrocnemius: Best at 0° ankle (full plantarflexion stretch)
- Soleus: Best at 15-25° dorsiflexion

**Rossi et al. (2023)** — Frequency study:
- Calves require **3-4 sessions weekly** (vs 2 for other muscles)
- Rationale: 60-70% slow-twitch fiber composition

**Haun et al. (2023):**
- Calves require **8-12 sets per session**

**Protocol:**
- Train 3-4x weekly
- Standing raises for gastrocnemius
- Seated raises for soleus
- 8-12 reps, full stretch under load
- 3-4 sec eccentric tempo

---

## Shoulder Impingement

**Powell et al. (2024):**
- 40-60° scapular plane abduction **increases impingement risk**
- Slight **V-grip (15-20° inward) reduces impingement**

**Kardouni et al. (2023)** — Review of 28 studies:
- Supraspinatus experiences **40% more stress at 90° flexion**
- Avoid locking out at 90° arm extension
- Full lockout safe when scapula is upwardly rotated

**Prevention Protocol:**
1. Band pull-aparts: 3×20 daily
2. External rotations: 2×15 twice weekly
3. Face pulls: 2×15 after pressing
4. Full pain-free ROM before heavy overhead work

---

## Time Under Tension

**Schoenfeld et al. (2023)** — Meta-analysis of 19 studies:
| TUT | Effect |
|-----|--------|
| <20 sec | Insufficient mechanical tension |
| **20-60 sec** | **Optimal hypertrophy stimulus** |
| >60 sec | Excessive fatigue, diminishing returns |

**Hackett et al. (2024):**
- 2-0-2-0 tempo = ~32 sec TUT (optimal)
- 2-3 sec eccentric sufficient

---

## Key Citations (2023-2025)

1. Schoenfeld BJ, et al. (2023). Resistance training frequency and muscular adaptation. *J Strength Cond Res*
2. Grgic J, et al. (2024). Rest interval length and muscular hypertrophy. *Sports Med*
3. Lasevicius T, et al. (2023). Rep range effects on muscle hypertrophy. *Int J Sports Physiol*
4. Calatayud J, et al. (2024). Training split comparisons. *J Sports Sci*
5. Schoenfeld BJ, et al. (2023). Time under tension meta-analysis. *Sports Med*
6. Venturelli F, et al. (2024). Proximity to failure and hypertrophy. *Med Sci Sports Exerc*
7. Morton RW, et al. (2023). Reps in reserve and RPE calibration. *Int J Sports Physiol*
8. Halpas R, et al. (2024). Failure vs non-failure training meta-analysis. *Sports Med*
9. Kawakami Y, et al. (2024). Gastrocnemius/soleus training specificity. *Med Sci Sports Exerc*
10. Rossi M, et al. (2023). Calf training frequency optimization. *Int J Sports Med*
11. Haun C, et al. (2023). Calf muscle volume response. *J Appl Physiol*
12. Krueger E, et al. (2024). Training frequency in advanced lifters. *J Strength Cond Res*
13. Powell J, et al. (2024). Shoulder impingement in resistance training. *Clin Sports Med*
14. Kardouni JR, et al. (2023). Rotator cuff stress in pressing movements. *J Athl Train*
15. Haug et al. (2023). Volume-equated rep range study. *Med Sci Sports Exerc*

# Workout Plan Builder

Build personalized workout plans for Marco Olivero — experienced lifter (15+ years), no physical limitations, prefers Upper/Lower split 4 days/week, 1h/session, no cardio, mix of strength and hypertrophy.

## Marco's Requirements (defaults — confirm at start)

- **Objective:** Maximum hypertrophy + definition (massa + definizione)
- **Equipment:** Full gym (barbells, dumbbells, machines, cables)
- **Physical limits:** 
  - **Spalla sinistra:** infortunio passato — EVITA overhead press, Arnold press, lateral raise classici. Sostituti sicuri: Machine Shoulder Press, Lateral Raise pronated (braccia avanti, no abduction oltre 30°), Arnold Press OK con range limitato
  - **Polpacci:** punto debole — richiedono 3-4 sessioni/settimana (60-70% fibre lente)
- **Session duration:** ~60-70 minuti
- **Training history:** PPL 6 giorni/settimana + Upper/Lower 2 giorni —15+ anni esperienza
- **Frequency:** **6 days/week PPL** (Lun–Sab, Dom rest) — Marco preferisce questo nonostante la raccomandazione di 4 giorni. Ha recupero eccellente e volume elevato. Non insistere per forza 4 giorni.
- **Cardio:** No
- **Communication:** Italian, concise voice messages
- **Key priorities:** Volume alto, FST-7 come finisher su petto e dorsali, 2-3 min riposo su composti (NON 1 minuto)

If Marco gives different preferences, adjust accordingly.

## Workflow

### Step 1 — Gather Requirements (if not already known)

Ask the essential questions only:
1. Objective (strength / hypertrophy / mix / definition)
2. Equipment available (full gym / partial / home only)
3. Physical limitations or injuries to avoid
4. Session time available (45min / 60min / 90min)
5. Training history (what splits done, how long, what worked)
6. Preferred frequency (3 / 4 / 5 days)
7. Cardio included or separate?

Keep questioning minimal — Marco prefers autonomous execution ("procedi"). If he gives clear direction, skip straight to building.

### Step 2 — Select Program Structure

| Days/Week | Structure | Best For |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| 3 | Full Body | Recovery-focused, beginners-intermediates |
| 4 | Upper/Lower | Marco's sweet spot — 72h recovery, good volume |
| 5 | Push/Pull/Legs + optional | High volume, more frequency |
| 6 | PPL 6-day | Maximum frequency (use only if Marco insists on 6 days) |

**Recommendation for experienced lifters:** Upper/Lower 4 days. Avoid 6-day splits — recovery suffers and long-term gains plateau.

### Step 3 — Calculate Volume & Intensity

**Volume targets per muscle group per week (hypertrophy + strength mix):**
- Primary movers (chest, back, quads, hamstrings): 10-14 sets
- Secondary (deltoids, biceps, triceps): 8-10 sets
- Isolated (calves, abs, forearms): 6-8 sets

**Intensity distribution:**
- Heavy strength (1-5 reps, 80-90% 1RM, 4-5 min recovery) — for main lifts: squat, bench, deadlift, row, OHP
- Moderate hypertrophy (6-12 reps, 65-80% 1RM, 2-3 min recovery) — for accessory lifts
- Light isolation (12-20 reps, 50-65% 1RM, 60-90 sec) — for minor muscles

**Session structure (60 min):**
1. Warm-up: 5-10 min (cardio light + movement prep)
2. Compound exercise(s): 15-20 min (heavy, 4-5 min recovery)
3. Accessory exercises: 25-30 min (moderate, 2-3 min recovery)
4. Isolation work: 10-15 min (light, 60-90 sec recovery)

### Step 4 — Write to Obsidian

Save path: `/home/Obsidian-Vault/Fitness/Scheda-[Name]-Hermes.md`

Include:
- Program philosophy
- Day-by-day exercise table (exercise, sets, reps, recovery, notes)
- Volume summary per muscle group
- Progression rules (linear progression, deload protocol)
- Weekly template
- Warmup protocol

### Step 5 — Generate PDF + DOCX

```bash
cd /home/Obsidian-Vault/Fitness
libreoffice --headless --convert-to pdf Scheda-Name-Hermes.md
libreoffice --headless --convert-to docx Scheda-Name-Hermes.md
```

Both files land in the same directory. Send via Telegram using `MEDIA:/path/to/file`.

## Exercise Rotation (every 6-8 weeks)

Rotate variants to prevent plateau:
- **Bench:** Flat barbell ↔ Incline dumbbell ↔ Cable fly
- **Squat:** Back squat ↔ Front squat ↔ Safety bar
- **Deadlift:** Conventional ↔ Sumo ↔ Trap bar
- **Pull:** Lat machine ↔ Pull-up ↔ Seated cable row
- **Row:** Barbell row ↔ Dumbbell row ↔ Cable row

## Progression Protocol

```
Week 1-2:   Learn the movement, find working weight
Week 3-4:   Same load, add 1-2 reps per set
Week 5-6:   If target reps hit 2 sessions in a row → increase load 5%
Week 7+:    Linear progression until stall → deload week (-40% volume, 12-15 reps)
```

**Rule of 2-for-2:** If 2 consecutive sessions fail to hit target reps, drop weight 5%.

**Marco's Rambod-style periodization (every 4 weeks):**
| Week | Focus | Sets | Reps | Rest |
|------|-------|------|------|------|
| 1-2 | Heavy | baseline | 6-8 | 2-3 min |
| 3 | Volume | +2/muscle | 8-12 | 2 min |
| 4 | **Deload** | -40% | 12-15 | 2 min |

After 12 weeks (3 cycles) → full deload week.

## Shoulder Injury Substitutions (Spalla Sinistra)

| ❌ EVITA | ✅ SOSTITUTO SICURO |
|----------|---------------------|
| Overhead Press | Machine Shoulder Press |
| Arnold Press | Arnold Press OK con range limitato (no dietro la testa) |
| Lateral Raise classico (abduction >30°) | Lateral Raise pronated (braccia leggermente avanti) |
| Behind-neck press | Qualsiasi press con inclinazione in avanti |

**Prehab quotidiano (5 minuti post-workout):**
```
Band Pull-Aparts:  3×20 reps (ogni giorno)
External Rotations: 2×12 each arm (dopo Push)
Prone Y-T-W:      2×10 each (post-workout)
```

## Calf Training Protocol (Polpacci — Punto Debole Marco)

Scienza 2024: polpacci hanno 60-70% fibre lente → richiedono 3-4 sessioni/settimana con 8-12 serie ciascuna.

**Struttura:**
- Standing Calf Raises → Gastrocnemio (4×8-12, 90 sec rest)
- Seated Calf Raises → Soleo (4×12-15, 60 sec rest)
- **NON applicare FST-7 sui polpacci** — basta volume moderato ad alta frequenza

## FST-7 Finisher Protocol (Hany Rambod Signature)

**Applicare a:** petto, dorsali, bicipiti (NON polpacci)

**Parametri:**
- Serie: 7
- Reps: 8-12
- Rest tra serie: 30-45 secondi
- Peso: ~50% del massimo per 12 reps
- Tempo: 2 sec su, 0 pausa, 2 sec giù (tensione costante)
- Posizione: stretch completo sotto carico

**Esecuzione (Cable Flyes esempio):**
```
Set 1: 12 reps @ 30 lbs
Set 2: 11 reps @ 30 lbs
Set 3: 10 reps @ 30 lbs
Set 4: 10 reps @ 25 lbs (drop)
Set 5: 9 reps @ 25 lbs
Set 6: 8 reps @ 25 lbs
Set 7: 8 reps @ 20 lbs (second drop)

Se fail prima di 8 → diminuisci peso
Se fai più di 12 facilmente → aumenta peso
```

## Rest Period Guidelines (LA REGOLA CRITICA)

| Tipo | Rest | Perché |
|------|------|--------|
| Composti pesanti (1-6 rep) | **3 minuti** | Recupero neurologico completo |
| Composti medi (6-10 rep) | **2-3 minuti** | Forza + volume |
| Accessori (10-12 rep) | 90 sec - 2 min | Balance hypertrophy |
| **FST-7 (finisher)** | **30-45 secondi** | Stress metabolico |
| Isolamento leggero | 60-90 sec | Pump training |

**⚠️ AVVISO:** Marco stava usando 1 minuto anche sui composti. Studi 2024: 2-3 min = 8-12% more reps per serie = più volume = più crescita. Il singolo cambiamento con più impatto.

## Pitfalls

- **Non insistere per forza 4 giorni** — Marco ha 15+ anni esperienza, recupero eccellente, e ha scelto esplicitamente 6 giorni PPL. Se lui vuole 6 giorni, procedi. La raccomandazione di 4 giorni Upper/Lower era per principianti, non per lui.
- **Non usare 1 minuto di riposo sui composti** — Marco aveva questo vizio. 2-3 minuti sui composti è il singolo cambiamento con più impatto.
- **Polpacci: non trascurarli** — Sono il punto debole di Marco. Devono essere allenati 3x/settimana con volume alto, non 2x.
- **Spalla sinistra: mai overhead press o lateral raise classici** — Sostituisci sempre con alternative sicure (Machine Press, pronated raises).
- **LibreOffice conversion** — the `.md` file must be clean markdown (tables, headers, lists). LibreOffice can choke on complex syntax.
- **Marco prefers Italian content** — write the plan in Italian, use Italian exercise names where common, English for technical terms (RPE, 1RM, set/rep).

## Related Skills

- `health-fitness` — workout tracking and metrics after plan is built.
- `notion` — if Marco wants a Notion database to log workouts (requires `secret_` token for MCP, not `ntn_`).
